subtopics.title: Definition of Economics
common_misconceptions: Economics is only about money
Scarcity means shortage
Robbins' definition ignores welfare aspect
subtopics.teaching_note: Key definitions: Adam Smith - 'Science of wealth.' Alfred Marshall - 'Study of mankind in the ordinary business of life.' Lionel Robbins - 'Science which studies human behaviour as a relationship between ends and scarce means which have alternative uses.' Scarcity is the central economic problem - unlimited wants vs limited resources.

subtopics.title: Basic Economic Concepts
common_misconceptions: Opportunity cost is only monetary
Points inside PPC represent efficiency (they represent inefficiency)
Economic growth shifts PPC inward
subtopics.teaching_note: Opportunity cost: value of the next best alternative foregone. Scale of preference: ranking of wants in order of importance. PPC: shows maximum combinations of two goods producible with given resources. Points ON curve = efficient. INSIDE = inefficient. OUTSIDE = unattainable. Shift outward = economic growth (more resources or better technology).

subtopics.title: Economic Systems
common_misconceptions: Pure capitalism and pure socialism exist in practice
Mixed economy means equal mix of both
Nigeria is purely capitalist
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Nigeria operates a mixed economy. Before SAP (1986): more socialist leaning with state-owned enterprises (NITEL, NEPA, NNPC). Post-SAP: privatization and commercialization. Features: private sector dominance with government regulation. Government provides infrastructure, education, healthcare while private sector drives production.

subtopics.title: Factors of Production
common_misconceptions: Land only means soil (it includes all natural resources)
Capital only means money (it includes manufactured tools/machinery)
Entrepreneur is just a business owner
subtopics.teaching_note: Land: all natural resources. Reward = rent. Characteristics: fixed supply, no cost of production, subject to diminishing returns. Labour: human effort (mental and physical). Reward = wages. Capital: man-made goods used to produce other goods. Reward = interest. Entrepreneur: organizes other factors, bears risk, makes decisions. Reward = profit.

subtopics.title: Production Function and Laws of Returns
common_misconceptions: Law of diminishing returns applies to all industries (only short run)
TP falling means MP is negative
AP maximum = MP maximum
subtopics.teaching_note: Production function: Q = f(L, K) - relationship between inputs and output. TP = total output. AP = TP/L. MP = ΔTP/ΔL. Law of diminishing returns: as more variable input is added to fixed input, MP eventually decreases. Stage 1: AP increasing. Stage 2: AP decreasing, TP still increasing (rational producer operates here). Stage 3: TP decreasing.

subtopics.title: Types of Costs
common_misconceptions: Fixed costs never change (they don't vary with output)
MC intersects AC at AC minimum (TRUE - emphasize this)
Average cost = marginal cost always
subtopics.teaching_note: Key relationships: TC = FC + VC. AC = TC/Q. MC = ΔTC/ΔQ. When MC < AC, AC is falling. When MC > AC, AC is rising. When MC = AC, AC is at minimum. This is a crucial relationship for exam questions. Short run: at least one fixed input. Long run: all inputs variable.

subtopics.title: Forms of Business Organization
common_misconceptions: Private company can sell shares to public (NO)
Sole proprietorship has limited liability (NO - unlimited)
All partnerships have unlimited liability (limited partnerships exist)
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: CAC (Corporate Affairs Commission) registers businesses. Sole proprietorship: one owner, unlimited liability, easy to form. Partnership: 2-20 partners, Deed of Partnership. Limited liability company: separate legal personality. Private: ltd, 2-50 members, restricts share transfer. Public: PLC, minimum 7 members, can sell shares to public. Cooperative: common economic interest. Public corporation: government-owned (NNPC, NPA, FAAN).

subtopics.title: Types of Market Structures
common_misconceptions: Perfect competition exists in reality (it's theoretical)
Monopoly always charges highest possible price
Oligopoly and monopoly are the same
subtopics.teaching_note: Comparison: Perfect competition: many buyers/sellers, homogeneous product, free entry/exit, no market power. Monopoly: single seller, barriers to entry, price maker, unique product. Monopolistic: many sellers, differentiated products, some pricing power. Oligopoly: few sellers, interdependent decisions, barriers to entry. Nigerian examples: NERC-regulated electricity (monopoly elements), MTN/Airtel/Glo (oligopoly in telecoms), open markets near perfect competition.

subtopics.title: Money - Definition, Types and Functions
common_misconceptions: Money must be backed by gold (fiat money is not)
Anything used as money IS money (must be generally acceptable)
Inflation means prices of all goods rise equally
subtopics.teaching_note: Functions of money: medium of exchange (solves barter's double coincidence problem), measure of value (common denominator), store of value (saves purchasing power), standard of deferred payment (enables borrowing/lending). Characteristics: portability, durability, divisibility, recognizability, stability of value. Nigerian currency: Naira (₦) and kobo. CBN is the sole issuer.

subtopics.title: Financial Institutions and Banking
common_misconceptions: CBB lends directly to individuals (it lends to commercial banks)
Commercial banks create money (they do - through lending)
All banks perform the same functions
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: CBN (established 1958, Act 2007): issues currency, banker to government, banker's bank, lender of last resort, foreign reserve management, monetary policy. Commercial banks: First Bank, UBA, GTBank, Zenith, Access. Deposit money banks accept deposits and give loans. Monetary policy tools: open market operations, cash reserve ratio (CRR), liquidity ratio (LR), monetary policy rate (MPR).
